content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 8, 1976


Donald A. Rhodes
District Superintendent
San Lorenzo Valley Unified Schools
Post Office Box 398
Ben Lomond, CA 95005

Dear Mr. Rhodes:

Governor Brown has provided our agency with a copy of
your letter of September 16, 1976, transmitting a resolution
adopted by the board of your school district. Essentially,
the resolution asks the Legislature to amend the Political
Reform Act of 1974 to exempt elected school board members from
the financial disclosure provisions of Government Code Sections
67100, et seq.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education, as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
interested school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests. Only those financial interests which
could be affected by the filer's official actions must be
disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest and provide for their disclosure. Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure will take an
active role in the design of each code's disclosure requirements,
so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a minimum
consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing
conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322 5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322 5901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 6441

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only a few very faint marks visible at the top. I cannot reliably transcribe meaningful text from this document as the content is not legible enough to read accurately.

Donald A. Rhodes
October 8, 1976
Page Two


     The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

          Assets and income of public officials which
          may be materially affected by their official
          actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
          circumstances the officials should be dis-
          qualified from acting in order that conflicts
          of interest may be avoided.

                                   Gov. Code § 81002(g).

     Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I firmly disagree with
the proposal. School boards make decisions of great consequence
to their communities. Like other public officials, school
board members serve in a position of public trust, and the
people have the right to be assured that their decisions will
be made free from bias caused by their own financial interests.
Disclosure of any financial interests which could foreseeably
be affected by their official actions is a reasonable and
effective means of preventing conflicts of interest. In recent
years the California Supreme Court and the courts of many
other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements are
valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of the
official is justified by the important public interest that is
served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

                                   Sincerely,


                                   Michael Bennett
                                   Executive Director

[The majority of the document is too faint or illegible to transcribe with confidence. There appears to be some typewritten or printed text in the middle and lower portions of the yellow page, but it is not clear enough to read accurately.]

SAN LORENZO VALLEY UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT

DR. DONALD A. RHODES
Superintendent
(408) 338-2241

WILLIAM F. MITCHELL
Asst. Superintendent
Business Services
(408) 338-2201

POST OFFICE BOX 398
BEN LOMOND, CALIFORNIA
95005

September 16, 1976

The Honorable Edmund G. Brown, Jr.
Governor, State of California
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Governor Brown:

Attached is a resolution approved unanimously by our Board
of Trustees. The purpose of the resolution is to bring to
your attention the imposition of the "Political Reform Act
of 1974" as it infringes upon the rights of school board
members and their immediate families in terms of an invasion
of personal privacy.

We and other boards stand to lose untold numbers of board
members who have served devotedly and who have given generously of their time to help us in the operation of our
school districts.

Any assistance that you can give in seeing that this Act
is revised to exclude school board members would be deeply
appreciated.

My regards,

Donald A. Rhodes
District Superintendent
SAN LORENZO VALLEY UNIFIED SCHOOLS

attachment

gb

[illegible]

SAN LORENZO VALLEY UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
Ben Lomond, California

CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government Code
Sections 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district Conflict
of Interest Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law to file
a disclosure statement disclosing their interest and those of
their immediate families in investments, real property, and
income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for public
scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.) and
Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 18700 et seq.)
and the Education Code (Section 1174) provide the needed legal
control of board member actions, and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an infringement
on the rights of private citizens and their immediate families
and is an invasion of personal privacy of board members and their
immediate families, and

WHEREAS, this act can require personal expenses for attorney and accountant
fees by board members holding non-paying elective board positions,
and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of this Code will encourage
private investigative snooping by persons bent on personal
vendettas, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of the California "Political
Reform Act of 1974" by boards will result in the resignation of
untold numbers of board members throughout the State, and

WHEREAS, school districts and communities of the State need citizens with
financial solvency, business acumen, and personal stability to
serve as board members.

IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Trustees of the SAN LORENZO
VALLEY UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT to publicly state its opposition to the
inclusion of elected public school board members within the requirements
of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" and it is resolved to
urge its legislators to take the necessary action to bring about the
changes needed to exclude school board members from the requirements of
the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" and it is resolved to
publicize said resolution to all other public school boards in the State
of California.

Adopted by Governing Board Sept. 8, 1976

8/23/76

The overall layout suggests this is a formal document or letter, but the content cannot be accurately determined from this image.]

embedding.qa_text: This is a letter from the Fair Political Practices Commission of the State of California, dated October 8, 1976, addressed to Donald A. Rhodes, District Superintendent of San Lorenzo Valley Unified Schools. The letter discusses Governor Brown's resolution adopted by the board of the school district, which asks the Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt elected school board members from the financial disclosure provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seq. The Commission explains that unpaid school board members believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain of their private financial interests. However, under existing law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel within the school districts "make or participate in the making of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable to members and officials who are capable of affecting such financial interests. Only those financial interests which could be affected by the filer's official actions must be disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts of interest and provide for their disclosure. The Commission encourages individuals who object to overly broad disclosure requirements to take an active role in the design of each code's disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a minimum consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest. The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies in drafting codes. In particular, they urge agencies to avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as possible the particular types of financial interests which could be affected by official decisions and which therefore must be disclosed.
